2025 · ICLR · Agents

RefactorBench: Evaluating Stateful Reasoning in Language Agents Through Code

100 hand-built, multi-file refactoring tasks that isolate one specific agent weakness — keeping track of what you've already changed across a long edit trajectory. GPT-4 agents solve 22% of base tasks where a human solves 87%, and feeding the agent an explicit running summary of its own edits recovers a 43.9% relative gain.

Dhruv Gautam, Spandan Garg, Jinu Jang, Neel Sundaresan, Roshanak Zilouchian Moghaddam · UC Berkeley & Microsoft
venue ICLR 2025 citations 27 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Coding-agent evaluation has converged on one shape of task: SWE-bench-style GitHub issues, where the agent reproduces a bug and patches it. That format is the strongest open-source signal we have, but it is also narrow. Because real GitHub snapshots are noisy, the benchmark pipeline filters heavily for issues with clean reproduction scripts and passing test suites — and that filtration skews the surviving tasks toward localized, reproducible bug-fixes. Whole categories of everyday software work fall outside it.

Refactoring is the obvious gap. A refactor is not a bug-fix: there is no failing test to chase, no stack trace to localize, and the "right answer" is a structural transformation that ripples across many files. Function-level code benchmarks like HumanEval/Codex sometimes include refactors, but only as isolated single-function edits — exactly the part models are already good at. What no benchmark measured was the hard part of agentic refactoring: composing many small, interdependent edits across a large codebase while remembering what you have already done.

The authors argue this exposes a distinct and under-tested capability — stateful reasoning. An LM agent is, formally, a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP): it sees only fragments of the repository through its tools, and after dozens of actions its internal model of "what state is the codebase in now" drifts away from reality. SWE-bench mostly rewards finding the one right place to edit; it does not stress an agent's ability to track an accumulating sequence of its own changes. RefactorBench is built to isolate precisely that.

// how it works

The method

RefactorBench is 100 handcrafted multi-file refactoring tasks spread over 9 popular open-source Python repositories (Django, Salt, Scrapy, Celery, Ansible, Tornado, Requests, Flask, FastAPI), chosen for their differing file structures. Each task asks the agent to produce a patch that makes the repository obey a specified refactor — say, rename a helper everywhere it is used, or extract a shared abstraction — and success is all-or-nothing: every test crafted for that task must pass.

The benchmark was built with a deliberately labor-intensive four-step pipeline. (1) Localization: GPT-4o was prompted with whole files plus Martin Fowler's refactoring catalog to suggest opportunities, then humans manually filtered to changes that genuinely span multiple files. (2) Reference solutions: experienced Python programmers handcrafted the edits, while GPT-4o verified each core sub-edit was tractable for a frontier model. (3) AST tests: for every verified sub-edit the developers wrote a unit test that walks the file's abstract syntax tree and checks the resulting code structure — deliberately not exact-line matching, so an agent gets credit for any correct realization of the change. (4) Instructions: a human-written base instruction, plus auto-generated lazy (terse, underspecified, like a real user) and descriptive (exhaustive, naming the files to touch — a near-upper-bound) variants.

Two design choices make the benchmark a clean probe rather than just another coding test. First, every task is multi-file by construction (single-file refactors were filtered out): reference solutions edit 2 to 31 files, 4.3 on average, which immediately defeats single-shot models and forces feedback-based agents to reason across files. Second, the AST tests are split into subtasks (2 to 27 per task, 6.51 on average), so a failure is interpretable — you can see exactly which file or which structural change the agent missed, rather than a single opaque pass/fail. Because each core edit was tractability-verified at construction time, a failure signals weak stateful reasoning between function calls, not an inability to write the code in the first place.

RefactorBench statisticMeanMax
Lazy instruction length (words)16.038
Base instruction length (words)20.644
Descriptive instruction length (words)68.8116
Codebase size (# files)2,327.66,815
Codebase size (# lines)304K1.8M
Reference solution (# files edited)4.331
AST tests (# functions)6.527
AST tests (# lines)131.1378
// what they found

The results

The headline gap is stark. Running a prompt-adjusted SWE-agent — the strongest open-source agent on SWE-bench at the time, with its internal prompts retuned away from bug-fixing so it does not treat a rename as a bug to reproduce — GPT-4 solves 12% of lazy, 22% of base, and 27% of descriptive tasks. Swapping in Claude-3.5-Sonnet lifts the descriptive number to 35%, the best baseline reported. A single proficient human developer, given IDE access and just five minutes per task on the base instructions, solves 87%. The authors treat these prompt-adjusted agents as an upper bound on current systems, since off-the-shelf agents do even worse by mistaking refactors for bug-fixes.

System / instructionResolution rate
SWE-agent · gpt-4 · lazy12.0%
SWE-agent · gpt-4 · base22.0%
SWE-agent · gpt-4 · descriptive27.0%
SWE-agent · claude-3.5-sonnet · descriptive35.0%
Human developer · base · 5 min87.0%

Two structural findings make the case that the bottleneck is state, not code. Tasks requiring edits in more than 6 files were never solved in any baseline — performance collapses as the number of interdependent changes grows. And when the authors took 3 already-solved descriptive tasks in a repo and concatenated their prompts into one longer "pseudotask," the agent — which could solve each individually — solved none of the combined instances. Composition, not capability, is where it breaks.

Three failure modes

From large-scale manual trajectory review (then scaled up with GPT-4 classifying unresolved runs against reference diffs — covering ~58% of failures, with a human reviewer agreeing ~74% of the time on a held-out set), the paper isolates three recurring failures:

  • Failing to localize and edit. Even with descriptive instructions that name every file to change, agents left the target file untouched on about 44% of the tests checking for a change — a sharp contrast with prior SWE-bench findings that agents usually edit the right place and fail only on implementation.
  • Intolerance of error states. 78.4% of trajectories error in a code-editing step. Many refactors require passing through a temporarily broken intermediate state (you rename in one file before fixing its callers), but agents — and the in-edit linting guardrails most open-source systems rely on — reject those edits, so a normal human workflow becomes a dead end.
  • Context flooding & in-context reward hacking. A single linting-error edit dumps an average of ~1,466 tokens of error-handling boilerplate into the context. That repetition crowds out the original goal: the agent latches onto the local "fix the syntax error" objective and, in some trajectories, simply ends the run once the editing loop resolves — having lost the refactor it was sent to do.

The fix: make the agent state-aware

The authors first confirm the underlying mechanism in a clean synthetic setup. They emulate a web agent tracking 15 preference categories, feed it a growing list of updates, and ask it to report the final state. Across 125 random runs, accuracy decays almost linearly with the number of actions — Figure 4 shows it sliding from 100% at the start to under 30% by step 50. The POMDP intuition holds: the more an agent acts, the worse its grip on the current state.

The remedy is an interface change, not a new model. They add an externally-computed state variable σ to the POMDP — a cached, continuously-updated natural-language summary of every edit the agent has made so far — and inject it before each function call. The trajectory the model conditions on becomes:

τN = ( a1, ω1, σ1, … , aN, ωN, σN ) // each step now carries an explicit running state σ, not just the action a and partial observation ω

Generalized one level further, a state update policy πstate : (τN, σN−1, …, σ0) → σN manages this summary for an entire digital environment — even folding in changes made by a concurrent external agent, so the LM can later choose to inspect an edit it didn't make. The agent no longer has to reconstruct "where am I now" from scratch each turn; the interface hands it that state directly.

The payoff is consistent across all three instruction sets. The state-aware SWE-agent lifts resolution rates from 12 / 22 / 27 to 18 / 31 / 38 (lazy / base / descriptive; the absolutes read from Figure 6) — the paper's prose states the headline as a 43.9% average relative increase over the instruction sets, plus a 71% average increase in subtask completion, the metric the AST subtask split was designed to surface. Passing more subtasks is exactly the signature of stronger stateful reasoning.

The paper also reports an updated baseline: SWE-agent 1.0 with GPT-4o holds the pattern (21% base, 31% descriptive) but slashes cost to $1.69 per successful instance (down from a $10 per-instance cap), and the authors note preliminary evidence that reasoning-model agents solve many more RefactorBench tasks.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

RefactorBench is a genuinely sharp instrument — the failure modes are specific, reproducible, and clearly tied to an interface defect rather than vague "model weakness." But the marquee numbers need reading with care, and on the most-quoted contrast the authors themselves are already half-walking it back.

The biggest caveat is that the headline gap rests on dated models. The 22%-vs-87% punchline is a GPT-4 agent against one human; the strongest model in the main results is Claude-3.5-Sonnet at 35%. The paper carries an explicit "these baselines are outdated" note, and its own updated section shows SWE-agent 1.0 already cuts cost ~6× while holding accuracy — plus a "preliminary" remark that reasoning-model agents solve "many more" tasks. So the gap is best read as a snapshot of agent interfaces circa early-2024, not a fixed measure of the frontier.

The human anchor is n=1: a single "proficient" developer with five minutes per task. There is no skill distribution, no inter-annotator agreement, and no sense of variance — yet "87%" is the number that frames the whole paper. Similarly, the state-aware win is reported as a relative +43.9% over a small base; the absolute deltas are real but modest (e.g. 27→38 on descriptive), and the figure caption says both agents use gpt-4 while the Section 6.1 text says the state-aware agent uses gpt-4o — an unresolved base-model ambiguity that muddies the attribution. A minor reporting slip compounds it: the base resolution rate appears as 22% in the abstract and Table 2, but 18% in the Section 4.2 text.

Finally, the failure-mode prevalence (58%) is itself LLM-graded — GPT-4 classifying trajectories against reference diffs, validated only by ~74% human agreement — so the taxonomy's quantitative weight inherits judge noise, even if the qualitative modes are convincing.

baselines

GPT-4 and Claude-3.5-Sonnet only; the authors concede they are "outdated," and SWE-agent 1.0 plus reasoning models already close much of the gap. No frontier reasoning models (o1/o3-class) in the main tables.

contamination

Tasks live in highly popular public Python repos the models very likely trained on. Gold solutions are withheld (only AST tests released) to limit overfitting, but the repos themselves are public, and "tractability" filtering curates tasks to be solvable by GPT-4o at construction — so difficulty is partly an artifact of the construction model.

what's missed

A +43.9% relative headline hides modest absolute gains; the human ceiling is one developer; failure-mode statistics are LLM-graded; with 100 tasks each instance ≈1%, so cross-model rate differences carry wide, unreported confidence intervals.

would raise confidence

Frontier reasoning models in the main results; a multi-annotator human baseline with a time/skill distribution and CIs; a fixed-model, absolute-delta state-aware comparison; held-out private repos to control contamination; a larger task pool.

// bottom line

A well-constructed, deliberately narrow probe of stateful, multi-file reasoning that surfaces three crisp, real agent failures — losing the edit location, refusing necessary error states, and drowning in context. Treat the "22% vs 87%" gap as a dated diagnostic of agent interface design, not a current capability score; the lasting contribution is the demonstration that an explicit, externally-maintained state representation buys a real improvement without touching the model.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. RefactorBench reframes coding-agent evaluation around multi-file refactoring — 100 handcrafted tasks across 9 Python repos, graded by AST subtask tests with withheld reference solutions — to isolate stateful reasoning, the capability SWE-bench-style bug-fixing leaves untested.
  2. Agents fail at composition, not code: a prompt-adjusted SWE-agent solves 22% of base tasks (vs a human's 87%), never solves a task needing >6 file edits, and can't solve three concatenated tasks it solves individually — pointing to an eroding internal model of cumulative state.
  3. The fix is an interface, not a bigger model: injecting an explicit, continuously-updated natural-language summary of the agent's own edits (σ in the POMDP) yields a 43.9% relative gain and a 71% lift in subtask completion — but the dated baselines mean the absolute gap to the frontier is smaller than the headline suggests.
